title: The Disastrous First Proposal (Hunsford)
book_context: Chapter 34 of Jane Austen's 'Pride and Prejudice'. Elizabeth is at Hunsford Parsonage visiting Charlotte. She has just learned from Colonel Fitzwilliam that Darcy was the reason Bingley abandoned Jane — she is, in this moment, privately furious with him. She thinks him proud, cruel, and the ruin of her sister's happiness. She also still believes Wickham's slanders against him. Darcy has spent weeks convinced his feelings for her are a weakness he should conquer; here he finally surrenders — but his proposal reveals he has been dwelling on the social inferiority of her family, the degradation he is risking by loving her. He is passionate AND insulting in the same breath — genuinely moved, but unable to hide his pride. Elizabeth's reply is cold, deliberate, the floodgates of months of held-back contempt opening — not hot rage, but the kind of slow, lethal indictment that lands every word. It is the emotional inverse of the Final Reconciliation scene.

characters.name: narrator
characters.personality: Austen-dry, literary, ironic yet warm, measured pace
sample_lines: Elizabeth stood astonished, she coloured, doubted, and was silent.
He spoke well, but there were feelings, besides those of the heart, to be detailed.

characters.name: Darcy
characters.personality: proud but vulnerable, formal, restrained fervor, self-possessed but exposed in love
sample_lines: In vain have I struggled. It will not do.
You must allow me to tell you how ardently I admire and love you.

characters.name: Elizabeth
characters.personality: sharp, witty, self-aware, quick intelligence — here icy, deliberate, lethal
sample_lines: I had not known you a month before I felt that you were the last man in the world whom I could ever be prevailed on to marry.
From the very beginning, from the first moment I may almost say, of my acquaintance with you,

emotion.label: formal
emotion.notes: Chapter heading; slight gravitas foreshadowing what's to come.

lines.speaker: narrator
lines.text: After months of tension, Darcy proposes in a manner that manages to be both passionate and deeply insulting.

emotion.label: dry
emotion.notes: Scene setter with a wry edge — Austen loves this kind of double-edged framing.

emotion.label: urgent
emotion.notes: He has been fighting himself for weeks. This is the dam breaking. Slight forward lean in pace — he is agitated, not composed.

emotion.label: firm
emotion.notes: Clipped, frustrated. He has conceded defeat to his own feelings.

lines.speaker: Darcy
lines.text: My feelings will not be repressed.

emotion.label: urgent
emotion.notes: The declaration. Each word has to be unmistakable.

lines.speaker: Darcy
lines.text: You must allow me to tell you how ardently I admire and love you.

emotion.label: vulnerable
emotion.notes: The peak. Note the tell: 'You must allow me' is imperious — even at his most exposed, he commands. But 'ardently I admire and love you' must land with genuine fervor, slowest on 'ardently' and 'love you'. This is the line where she could, in another world, say yes.

lines.speaker: narrator
lines.text: Elizabeth stood astonished, she coloured, doubted, and was silent.

emotion.label: calm
emotion.notes: The rhythm of the four verbs matters — each a distinct beat of her reaction. Austen-dry observation.

lines.speaker: narrator
lines.text: This he considered sufficient encouragement, and immediately followed it by avowing hope and fear, complaint and resentment.

emotion.label: wry
emotion.notes: The irony is thick — 'this he considered sufficient encouragement' is Austen laughing at Darcy's self-regard. Deliver with controlled wryness.

lines.speaker: narrator
lines.text: He spoke well, but there were feelings, besides those of the heart, to be detailed, and he was not more eloquent on the subject of tenderness than of pride.

emotion.label: dry
emotion.notes: The indictment. This is Austen's scalpel — 'not more eloquent on the subject of tenderness than of pride' is the line that tells us Darcy is already sabotaging himself. Measured, the emphasis lives in the balance.

emotion.label: formal
emotion.notes: Brief narrative handoff; holds a small breath before she speaks.

lines.speaker: Elizabeth
lines.text: From the very beginning, from the first moment I may almost say, of my acquaintance with you,

emotion.label: commanding
emotion.notes: Cold opening. Each phrase deliberately set down. Not hot — lethal. The repetition 'from the very beginning, from the first moment' is a scaffold she's building to mount her indictment.

lines.speaker: Elizabeth
lines.text: your manners impressing me with the fullest belief of your arrogance, your conceit, and your selfish disdain of the feelings of others...

emotion.label: angry
emotion.notes: The triad — arrogance / conceit / disdain — must each land. Building force, but still held. The ellipsis is a pause where she gathers for the killing blow. Do NOT rush.

lines.speaker: Elizabeth
lines.text: I had not known you a month before I felt that you were the last man in the world whom I could ever be prevailed on to marry.

emotion.label: resolute
emotion.notes: The kill shot. Every word weighted. Slowest on 'the last man in the world' and 'ever be prevailed on to marry'. This is the line that scars him for months and drives the letter and the whole arc of Volume III. Final as a closing door.
